tldr: Firebase (Firestore + Auth + Storage + Cloud Functions + RTDB) maps to Supabase (Postgres + Auth + Storage + Edge Functions + Realtime). Feature-equivalent for most apps, but it's a paradigm shift: NoSQL documents become relational tables, Security Rules become Postgres RLS, and FCM has no first-party replacement. Plan a multi-week migration with a parallel-run cutover.
why_switch: Postgres + SQL: real joins, transactions, complex queries, CTEs — replaces Firestore's limited query model.
Row Level Security (RLS) is declarative SQL on the same engine that stores data, not a separate rules language to test.
Open source and self-hostable — you can run the entire Supabase stack on your own infra if you ever leave the managed cloud.
Predictable freemium pricing ($25/mo Pro per Supabase pricing page) vs. Blaze pay-as-you-go that mixes reads, writes, network, and Cloud Functions invocations.
Reduced Google Cloud lock-in: no forced GCP project, no Firebase-specific SDKs in clients (PostgREST + supabase-js are standard HTTP/WebSockets).
Standard tooling: pg_dump, psql, any Postgres client, any ORM (Prisma, Drizzle, Kysely) — vs. proprietary Firestore admin SDK.
Edge Functions run on Deno (open standard) instead of Cloud Functions' Node-only managed runtime tied to GCP.
what_to_export: Firestore collections and documents (per-collection export — subcollections require separate runs).
Firebase Authentication users including password hash parameters (algorithm, base64_signer_key, base64_salt_separator, rounds, mem_cost).
Realtime Database JSON tree (separate product from Firestore — export via Firebase console or REST API).
Cloud Storage for Firebase buckets (objects + metadata; ACLs do not transfer).
Cloud Functions source code (already in your repo — port logic, don't 'export' artifacts).
Firebase Hosting static assets and rewrite/redirect rules.
Firestore Security Rules and Storage Security Rules (as reference for writing RLS policies).
Firestore composite indexes (recreate as Postgres indexes).
Remote Config parameter values (no direct equivalent — model as a Postgres table or Edge Config).
FCM topics and device tokens (no Supabase equivalent — keep FCM, OneSignal, or roll your own).
Custom claims on Auth users (move to Supabase JWT app_metadata or a profiles table).
export_method: Firestore: enable billing (Blaze), then `gcloud firestore export gs://BUCKET_NAME [--collection-ids=col1,col2]` writes a managed export to a Cloud Storage bucket. The Cloud Firestore service agent (service-PROJECT_NUMBER@gcp-sa-firestore.iam.gserviceaccount.com) needs Storage Admin on the bucket. Note: collection-group exports do not automatically include subcollections, and indexes are not part of the export. For row-level migration, the supabase-community/firebase-to-supabase tooling uses `firestore2json.js` to pull collections directly via the Admin SDK into per-collection JSON files. Auth: `firebase auth:export users.json --format=json` (Firebase CLI) produces a JSON file with users plus a top-level `hash_config` block; cross-check the password-hash parameters in Firebase Console → Authentication → Users → ⋮ → Password hash parameters. Storage: use the firebase-to-supabase `download.js` script with an Admin SDK service-account key — it iterates objects and writes them to a local folder. `gsutil -m cp -r gs://BUCKET ./local` is the generic alternative. Cloud Functions: source already lives in your repository; rewrite as Supabase Edge Functions (Deno) or Postgres functions/triggers — there's nothing to 'export' from Google. Realtime DB: Firebase Console → Realtime Database → Export JSON, or `curl 'https://<db>.firebaseio.com/.json?auth=<secret>'`.
import_method: Database: design your Postgres schema first (this is the hardest step — Firestore is schemaless). Create tables with `supabase migration new` or raw SQL, then import. The supabase-community `json2supabase.js` script reads the per-collection JSON output of `firestore2json.js` and inserts rows; against a fresh table it auto-creates basic columns of type `text`, `numeric`, `boolean`, or `jsonb`. For complex nested documents, write a transform hook to split into normalized tables before import. Auth: use the companion `import_users` script — it inserts into the `auth.users` table preserving the Firebase scrypt hash and hash parameters so existing passwords keep working. Per Supabase docs, for advanced cases you can run a small middleware that verifies against Firebase scrypt on first login and re-hashes — see the firebase-to-supabase repo. Storage: run `node upload.js <prefix> <folder> <bucket>`; if the bucket doesn't exist it's created as non-public, and you must set policies on `storage.objects` in the Supabase Dashboard before clients can read. Edge Functions: `supabase functions new <name>` then `supabase functions deploy <name>`. RLS: rewrite each Security Rule as a Postgres policy on the matching table; test with `set role authenticated; set request.jwt.claims = '...'` against representative JWTs.

concept_mapping.target: Postgres table (or single jsonb column for unstructured docs)
Child table with foreign key to parent
Row Level Security (RLS) policies in SQL
Supabase Realtime channel on Postgres logical replication
Postgres + Supabase Realtime (or a single jsonb tree if you really want the original shape)
JWT `app_metadata` (set via Admin API) or a `profiles` table joined via `auth.uid()`
Supabase Storage (S3-compatible buckets with policies on `storage.objects`)
Supabase Edge Functions (Deno) or PostgREST RPC to a Postgres function
Postgres triggers + `pg_net`/`pg_cron`, or Database Webhooks → Edge Function
No first-party equivalent — keep FCM, or use OneSignal / Expo Push
No direct equivalent — model as a config table read by the client
Out of scope for Supabase — use Vercel, Netlify, or Cloudflare Pages

effort: 4-10 weeks for a real production app (one engineer, full-time). Breakdown: 1-2 weeks schema design and RLS rewrite, 1-2 weeks data migration scripts and dry runs, 1-2 weeks porting Cloud Functions to Edge Functions, 1-2 weeks client SDK swap and QA, 1-2 weeks parallel-run + cutover. A read-mostly app with one Firestore collection and Auth can be done in a long weekend; a Firestore-heavy app with deep subcollection nesting and dozens of Cloud Functions trends toward the 10-week end.
gotchas: Schema design is on you: Firestore is schemaless, Postgres is not. You must decide per-collection whether to normalize into tables or store as `jsonb`. Get this wrong and queries get painful later.

Password hashes: Firebase uses a custom scrypt variant (firebase-scrypt) that is NOT standard scrypt or bcrypt. Supabase preserves the hash + parameters on import so existing passwords keep working, but custom auth backends won't verify them without the firebase-scrypt algorithm — see the firebase-to-supabase middleware pattern.

Security Rules → RLS is not mechanical. Rules use `request.auth`, `resource.data`, and a custom DSL; RLS uses SQL with `auth.uid()` and table columns. Plan to rewrite each rule and test with both authenticated and anonymous JWTs.

Real-time semantics differ. `onSnapshot` delivers full document state on every change; Supabase Realtime delivers row-level INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE events from logical replication. Code that depended on 'always have the full doc' must adapt.
Subcollections do not auto-export. `gcloud firestore export` and `firestore2json.js` operate per collection; you must list and migrate subcollections explicitly.

Anonymous users: Firebase auto-creates anonymous UIDs that persist across sessions on a device. Supabase supports anonymous sign-ins (must be enabled in dashboard) but the UID generation rules differ — plan a linking strategy if you rely on persistent anon identity.
FCM has no Supabase equivalent. If you rely on push, keep Firebase Cloud Messaging as a standalone service or move to OneSignal / Expo Push — don't expect Supabase to replace it.

Cloud Functions vs. Edge Functions runtime gap: Cloud Functions are Node.js with full npm; Edge Functions run on Deno with a different module system, shorter execution limits, and no native Firebase Admin SDK. Heavy server logic may be better on a separate Node host that talks to Postgres directly.

Firestore queries don't translate 1:1. `array-contains-any`, `where in` with up to 30 values, and composite-index-backed queries map to SQL but performance characteristics differ — benchmark hot paths on real data volumes before cutover.

Firestore exports are not point-in-time snapshots by default — writes during the export may or may not be included. For a clean cutover either freeze writes or use PITR exports (whole-minute timestamps within the past seven days).

email.subject: Migrating from Firebase to Supabase: a practical playbook
email.body:
Hi,

If you're weighing a Firebase → Supabase move, here's the short version of what actually matters.

What you keep: your auth users (with working passwords, thanks to Firebase's exported hash parameters), your data (as Postgres tables or jsonb), your Storage objects, and your Cloud Functions logic (rewritten as Edge Functions or Postgres triggers).

What changes: Firestore's schemaless documents become a real schema you have to design. Security Rules become RLS policies in SQL. `onSnapshot` becomes row-level Realtime events. FCM has no Supabase equivalent — keep it or swap to OneSignal.

The path:
1. Design your Postgres schema first. This is the hard part.
2. Export Firestore (`gcloud firestore export` or `firestore2json.js`) and Auth (`firebase auth:export` plus hash parameters from the console).
3. Import with the supabase-community `firebase-to-supabase` scripts (`json2supabase.js`, `import_users`).
4. Rewrite Security Rules as RLS policies and test with real JWTs.
5. Port Cloud Functions to Edge Functions (Deno) or Postgres functions.
6. Run both stacks in parallel for a week, dual-write, then cut over reads.

Budget 4-10 weeks for a real production app. The wins: SQL, transactions, OSS, predictable pricing, no GCP lock-in.

Good luck,
